past_medical_history: Infantile epilepsy diagnosed at age 2, treated until age 10 with seizure freedom, medications weaned off
Seizure recurrence at age 17 prompting resumption of antiepileptic therapy
Polytherapy with valproate, phenobarbital, and carbamazepine for refractory focal seizures
Iron deficiency anemia, treated with oral supplementation

medications.indication: Supplementation due to antiepileptic drug use

family_history: Mother with history of febrile seizures in childhood
Paternal grandmother with epilepsy, poorly controlled
No family history of neurodegenerative disease or brain tumors

social_history.occupation: Office clerk, currently on medical leave
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a suburban apartment
social_history.driving: Does not drive due to ongoing seizures

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent in Japanese and conversational English without paraphasic errors. Naming and comprehension intact. Short-term recall 3/3 at registration and 2/3 at five minutes (missed one word despite category cueing). Mildly drowsy, consistent with post-ictal state approximately 4 hours after most recent witnessed seizure.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3.5 mm to 2 mm). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal. Tone mildly increased in right upper extremity compared to left but within functional range. Strength 5/5 throughout all extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements at rest.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin performed without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Lateral tongue bite noted on the right side, consistent with recent seizure. Patient reports a rising sensation in her epigastrium that precedes her habitual seizures.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent seizures breaking through triple antiepileptic drug therapy, with three convulsive episodes per week preceded by an intense rising sensation in the stomach and a feeling of deja vu
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Tanaka is a 29-year-old right-handed Japanese woman who is admitted to the inpatient epilepsy monitoring unit for evaluation of medically refractory focal seizures. She has a longstanding history of epilepsy dating back to infancy, with initial seizure onset at age 2. Her childhood seizures were controlled with medication, and she was weaned off antiepileptic therapy at age 10 after several years of seizure freedom. At age 17, she experienced recurrence of seizures characterized by a stereotyped aura consisting of an intense epigastric rising sensation accompanied by a vivid feeling of deja vu, followed by loss of awareness, during which witnesses describe her staring blankly, smacking her lips, and picking at her clothing with her right hand for approximately 30 to 60 seconds. She has no recollection of events during these episodes and requires several minutes to regain full awareness afterward.

Despite resumption of antiepileptic therapy, her seizures have proven refractory to multiple medication trials. She is currently on triple therapy with valproate 1400 mg per day, phenobarbital 140 mg per day, and carbamazepine 1200 mg per day. Even with therapeutic drug levels confirmed at a recent outpatient visit, she continues to experience approximately three convulsive episodes per week. Several of these events begin with her characteristic aura and progress to focal impaired awareness seizures with oral and manual automatisms. Approximately once weekly, the seizures generalize to bilateral tonic-clonic activity lasting 60 to 90 seconds, with post-ictal confusion lasting 20 to 40 minutes. She was brought to the emergency department after a witnessed bilateral tonic-clonic seizure at home approximately four hours ago, during which she was incontinent of urine and sustained a lateral tongue bite. Her husband reports that the semiology of today's event was identical to her habitual seizures. There has been no recent medication non-adherence, illness, fever, sleep deprivation, or new medications. She denies any preceding headache, visual changes, or focal weakness. She has been taking folic acid supplementation given her polytherapy regimen.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm attenuating with eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles observed during stage II sleep

findings.frequency: Frequent, occurring every 1-2 minutes during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness and sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with dipole field extending to T4 and F4, duration 30-70 ms, followed by after-going slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with an epileptogenic focus in the right anterior temporal region

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta activity lasting 4-10 seconds
findings.state: Predominantly during drowsiness and light sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: TIRDA is strongly associated with temporal lobe epilepsy and supports lateralization to the right temporal lobe

artifacts.impact: Minimal, did not obscure interictal abnormalities

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed; no provocation of electrographic seizure activity. Mild symmetric background slowing noted.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to frequent right anterior temporal sharp waves and spikes with phase reversal at F8 and intermittent right temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). These findings are highly consistent with a right temporal lobe epileptogenic focus. No electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Background and sleep architecture are otherwise normal.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording; no clinical events captured. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended to capture habitual events and confirm ictal onset localization.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual seizures and correlate semiology with electrographic changes
MRI brain with epilepsy protocol (3T preferred with thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes)
Review current AED levels and consider medication optimization

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume reduced by approximately 30% compared to the left

signal_characteristics.T1: Decreased volume and loss of normal internal architecture of the right hippocampus
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus relative to the left, most pronounced in body and head
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus, particularly the body and head segments

findings.borders: Well-defined volume loss without associated mass lesion

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 1.9 cm3 (below 3rd percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No other cortical or subcortical signal abnormalities
Ventricles and sulci normal in caliber for patient age
Mild right temporal horn dilation, consistent with ipsilateral hippocampal volume loss
mri.impression: Right mesial temporal sclerosis with significant hippocampal volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, most prominent in the hippocampal body and head. Mild right temporal horn prominence consistent with ex vacuo dilation. No mass lesion, acute infarction, or hemorrhage. Findings are concordant with a right temporal lobe epileptogenic focus.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low, no mass lesion or enhancement present

recommended_actions: Correlate with EEG findings for lateralization concordance
Consider FDG-PET if pre-surgical evaluation is pursued
Neuropsychological testing for lateralizing cognitive deficits

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes. No ECG features suggestive of a cardiac etiology for the patient's episodes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG makes a primary cardiac etiology unlikely.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly low hemoglobin, consistent with known iron deficiency anemia on treatment
Mildly low, consistent with iron deficiency anemia

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Within therapeutic range, but seizures persist despite adequate level
Within therapeutic range, near upper end

Additional.clinical_significance: Elevated prolactin drawn approximately 30 minutes after witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Post-ictal prolactin elevation supports epileptic seizure. Valproate can also mildly elevate prolactin but typically not to this degree.
Normal liver function; important to monitor given valproate use
Upper end of normal; valproate-associated hyperammonemia excluded

labs.interpretation: CBC shows mild microcytic anemia consistent with known iron deficiency. Basic metabolic panel is within normal limits, excluding metabolic causes for seizure exacerbation. All three antiepileptic drug levels are within therapeutic ranges, confirming medication adherence and adequate drug exposure despite ongoing seizures, consistent with drug-resistant epilepsy. Prolactin is elevated at 48.2 ng/mL, supporting a recent epileptic seizure. Liver enzymes and ammonia are normal, important for monitoring valproate safety. TSH normal.
abnormal_values_summary: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (low, reference 12.0-16.0) - known iron deficiency anemia
Hematocrit 35.4% (low, reference 36.0-46.0) - consistent with iron deficiency
MCV 78.5 fL (low, reference 80-100) - microcytic, consistent with iron deficiency

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture recorded over 5 days of continuous monitoring

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 15 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 30 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximum negativity at F8/T4, followed by after-going slow wave
findings.state: All states of consciousness, maximal during NREM sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Highly consistent with right temporal epileptogenic zone

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric
findings.frequency: Eleven clinical seizures captured over 5 days (consistent with approximately 3 per day)
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right anterior temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta activity evolving to 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta with spread to right parasagittal region over 40-60 seconds. Three of eleven seizures progressed to bilateral tonic-clonic activity.
findings.state: Seizures occurred during wakefulness and sleep. Most common during early morning hours.
findings.clinical_correlation: Seizure semiology: patient reports epigastric rising sensation and deja vu, followed by behavioral arrest, lip smacking, and right hand fumbling automatisms. Loss of awareness lasting 30-60 seconds. Three events progressed to focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures lasting 60-90 seconds with post-ictal confusion. Semiology and electrographic onset concordant with right mesial temporal lobe origin.

findings.morphology: Rhythmic 2-3 Hz delta activity in right temporal chain

artifacts.location: Generalized, during ictal events with bilateral tonic-clonic activity
artifacts.impact: Obscured late ictal EEG during bilateral tonic-clonic phases but seizure onsets clearly captured in all events

output.impression: Abnormal 5-day continuous video-EEG monitoring. Eleven habitual clinical seizures captured with consistent right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4) concordant with clinical semiology of temporal lobe seizures. Three events progressed to focal to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Frequent right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges. All seizures arose from the right temporal lobe with no independent contralateral onset. Findings confirm right temporal lobe epilepsy meeting ILAE criteria for drug-resistant epilepsy and support consideration for epilepsy surgery evaluation.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during bilateral tonic-clonic phases limited assessment of late ictal patterns. AEDs were reduced during monitoring, which may have increased seizure frequency.
recommended_actions: Epilepsy surgery evaluation given drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy with concordant findings
Neuropsychological evaluation for surgical planning and baseline cognitive assessment
FDG-PET for additional lateralizing and localizing data
Consider Wada test or fMRI for language lateralization if surgery is pursued

findings.location: Right hippocampus, most prominent in the head and body segments
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume 1.8 cm3 versus left 3.3 cm3, asymmetry ratio 0.29

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Loss of normal internal structure of the right hippocampus with reduced gray-white differentiation
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Markedly hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus on thin coronal cuts (3 mm slices perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal head and body, clearly abnormal compared to the left

findings.mass_effect: None. Mild ex vacuo dilation of the right temporal horn.
findings.borders: Well-defined atrophic changes without associated mass

signal_characteristics.overall: Normal signal throughout the remainder of the cerebral cortex, white matter, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

additional_observations: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol including thin coronal FLAIR and T2-weighted images perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus
No evidence of focal cortical dysplasia elsewhere
output.impression: High-resolution 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol confirms right mesial temporal sclerosis with marked right hippocampal atrophy and T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. No dual pathology or other structural abnormalities identified. Findings are concordant with right temporal lobe epilepsy.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Very low - no mass, no enhancement

recommended_actions: Correlate with video-EEG findings for concordance
Consider FDG-PET for additional lateralization data
Neuropsychological testing for lateralization of memory function

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Below average visual memory, consistent with right temporal lobe dysfunction
Impaired visual memory consolidation, lateralizing to right mesial temporal lobe dysfunction
Average verbal learning, suggesting relatively preserved left temporal lobe function

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates a material-specific visual memory deficit with relative preservation of verbal memory. This pattern lateralizes to right temporal lobe dysfunction and is concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis identified on MRI. Overall cognitive functioning is in the average range. The visual memory deficit is consistent with right hippocampal pathology. Adequate verbal memory reserve suggests left temporal lobe integrity, which is a favorable indicator if right-sided surgical intervention is considered.
abnormal_values_summary: Visual memory learning (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 35 - below average, lateralizing to right temporal dysfunction
Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 - impaired, consistent with right hippocampal pathology

findings.location: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, and amygdala) and moderately reduced uptake in the right lateral temporal neocortex compared to the left
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.76 (normal >0.90), indicating significant right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during EEG-confirmed interictal state
No areas of hypermetabolism to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. Multi-modal concordance strongly supports right temporal lobe epilepsy and is favorable for surgical candidacy.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy with mesial temporal sclerosis
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - concordant with MRI and EEG

recommended_actions: Multi-disciplinary epilepsy surgery conference for surgical candidacy discussion
Consider Wada test or fMRI for language and memory lateralization prior to surgery
Discuss surgical options including anterior temporal lobectomy and selective amygdalohippocampectomy

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Within therapeutic range. Seizures persist despite adequate levels, confirming drug resistance.
Within therapeutic range, near upper end.

output.interpretation: All three antiepileptic drug levels are within therapeutic ranges, confirming medication adherence and adequate drug exposure. Despite triple therapy at therapeutic levels, the patient continues to have approximately three seizures per week. This meets the ILAE definition of drug-resistant epilepsy (failure of two or more appropriately chosen and adequately dosed AEDs). Continued escalation of medical therapy is unlikely to achieve seizure freedom; surgical evaluation is warranted.

output.query: drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy mesial temporal sclerosis surgical outcomes polytherapy

results.title: Randomized controlled trial of early surgery for temporal lobe epilepsy (ERSET)
results.summary: Early surgical intervention for mesial temporal lobe epilepsy resulted in 73% seizure freedom versus 0% with continued medical therapy at 2 years.

results.title: Long-term seizure outcomes after epilepsy surgery
results.summary: Approximately 52% of patients remained seizure-free at 10 years following temporal lobe resection. Mesial temporal sclerosis and concordant pre-surgical evaluation were associated with favorable outcomes.

results.title: Valproate and teratogenicity: implications for women of childbearing age
results.summary: Valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk among AEDs (major congenital malformation rate 10-11%). It is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless no suitable alternative exists. Polytherapy regimens including valproate carry additive risk.

results.title: ILAE consensus on drug-resistant epilepsy
results.summary: Drug-resistant epilepsy is defined as failure of adequate trials of two tolerated, appropriately chosen, and used AED schedules to achieve sustained seizure freedom. Early referral for surgical evaluation is recommended.

output.summary: Current evidence strongly supports early referral for epilepsy surgery in patients with drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy and mesial temporal sclerosis. The ERSET trial demonstrated superior outcomes with surgery compared to continued medical therapy. For women of childbearing age, valproate poses significant teratogenic risk and should be replaced if possible. In this patient on triple therapy with ongoing seizures, surgical evaluation is the most evidence-based next step.

output.proposed: Current polytherapy: Valproate 1400 mg/day + Phenobarbital 140 mg/day + Carbamazepine 1200 mg/day
interactions: Valproate inhibits metabolism of phenobarbital, increasing phenobarbital levels and sedation risk
Carbamazepine induces hepatic enzymes (CYP3A4), reducing valproate levels and potentially reducing its efficacy
Valproate inhibits carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide hydrolase, increasing levels of the active and potentially toxic carbamazepine epoxide metabolite
Phenobarbital induces CYP enzymes, potentially reducing valproate levels
Triple enzyme-inducing and enzyme-inhibiting AED combination results in unpredictable pharmacokinetics
contraindications: Valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless no suitable alternative is available (high teratogenic risk with dose-dependent neural tube defect risk)
The combination of enzyme inducers and inhibitors creates complex pharmacokinetic interactions that make dose optimization extremely difficult
warnings: Valproate teratogenicity: dose-dependent risk, highest risk at >1000 mg/day. This patient is on 1400 mg/day.
Phenobarbital is a strong CNS depressant and enzyme inducer; contributes to sedation and cognitive impairment
Carbamazepine can cause hyponatremia, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (higher risk in Asian populations with HLA-B*1502), and bone marrow suppression
Triple therapy increases risk of cumulative CNS side effects (sedation, cognitive slowing, ataxia)
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500-1500 mg twice daily (no significant drug interactions, not teratogenic at recommended doses)
Lamotrigine 100-200 mg twice daily (must titrate slowly, lower teratogenic risk than valproate)
Lacosamide 100-200 mg twice daily (minimal interactions, well tolerated)
Given drug resistance, strongly consider surgical evaluation rather than further medication changes

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis, drug-resistant)

differential.key_features: Stereotyped semiology with classic temporal lobe aura (epigastric rising, deja vu), consistent automatisms, and post-ictal confusion. Elevated post-ictal prolactin and clear EEG abnormalities argue strongly against PNES.
No prodrome of lightheadedness. Prolonged post-ictal confusion inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. Tonic-clonic activity lasting 60-90 seconds far exceeds convulsive syncope duration.

Migraine aura spreads slowly over minutes and is typically visual or sensory. Patient's episodes are brief, include deja vu and epigastric rising (classic temporal lobe aura), and are followed by impaired awareness and automatisms.
Patient is young with no vascular risk factors. TIAs cause negative symptoms (weakness, numbness), not the positive phenomena and altered awareness seen in this case.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed seizure history including aura description, semiology, frequency, and witnesses' account
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Classic temporal lobe aura (deja vu, epigastric rising) followed by focal impaired awareness seizures with automatisms and secondary generalization
Largely normal interictal examination with lateral tongue bite from recent seizure and mild post-ictal memory impairment
Right anterior temporal sharp waves and spikes with TIRDA consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right hippocampal sclerosis with volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, AED levels, prolactin, and liver function tests
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, therapeutic AED levels despite ongoing seizures, elevated post-ictal prolactin, normal hepatic function

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac etiology
Review current antiepileptic drug regimen for interactions and appropriateness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Significant drug-drug interactions in current triple therapy, valproate teratogenicity risk in woman of childbearing age

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for surgical evaluation given drug-resistant epilepsy with concordant structural lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral for pre-surgical workup including prolonged video-EEG monitoring, neuropsychological testing, and FDG-PET

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient regarding teratogenic risks of valproate and the importance of contraception while on current regimen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient counseled on valproate teratogenicity and pregnancy prevention

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to characterize and lateralize the epileptogenic focus
Obtain brain MRI to identify the structural substrate (hippocampal sclerosis)
Confirm AED levels are therapeutic to establish drug resistance rather than non-adherence
Recognize this as drug-resistant epilepsy meeting ILAE criteria (failure of 3 appropriately dosed AEDs)
Refer for epilepsy surgery evaluation given concordant clinical, EEG, and MRI findings
Counsel woman of childbearing age about teratogenic risk of valproate
contraindicated_actions: Increasing valproate dose further in a woman of childbearing age without discussing teratogenic risks and alternatives
Performing lumbar puncture (no clinical indication; no signs of infection or inflammation)
Discharging without a plan for epilepsy surgery referral given drug-resistant epilepsy with a structural lesion
Adding another AED without considering surgical evaluation, as further polytherapy is unlikely to achieve seizure freedom
Prescribing medications that lower seizure threshold (e.g., bupropion, tramadol)
key_reasoning_points: The combination of epigastric rising sensation and deja vu is a classic mesial temporal lobe aura, localizing the seizure onset to the mesial temporal structures

History of infantile epilepsy with early seizure freedom and later recurrence is a classic trajectory for mesial temporal sclerosis, potentially related to an initial precipitating injury in early childhood
All three AED levels are within therapeutic ranges yet seizures persist at a frequency of 3 per week, establishing this as drug-resistant epilepsy per ILAE criteria
Concordance across modalities (semiology localizing to right temporal lobe, right temporal EEG abnormalities, right hippocampal sclerosis on MRI) is essential for surgical candidacy
Valproate at 1400 mg/day in a 29-year-old woman poses significant teratogenic risk, and the published source case demonstrated fetal malformations attributed to valproate exposure

Complex pharmacokinetic interactions between valproate (enzyme inhibitor), carbamazepine (enzyme inducer), and phenobarbital (enzyme inducer) make polytherapy optimization extremely difficult and support consideration of surgical cure rather than further medication manipulation
Elevated post-ictal prolactin supports the diagnosis of epileptic seizures and helps distinguish from psychogenic non-epileptic events

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic temporal lobe seizure semiology (epigastric rising aura, deja vu, automatisms), clear right hippocampal sclerosis on MRI, concordant right temporal EEG abnormalities, and established drug resistance on triple AED therapy. Minimal confounders; mild iron deficiency anemia is incidental.
